chunk_summary: Risk stratification for pediatric food allergy focuses on identifying likelihood of allergy, severity of reactions, and nutritional risks. High-risk features include prior anaphylaxis, poorly controlled asthma, and remote residence. Referral thresholds emphasize severe non-IgE phenotypes, failure to thrive, and supervised oral food challenges.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of eczema, atopic family history, and comorbidities in pediatric food allergy risk stratification.
Identify high-risk features that necessitate emergency preparedness and referral for food allergy management.
Learn referral triggers for severe non-IgE phenotypes and nutritional vulnerabilities in elimination diets.
Integrate asthma control and food allergy action plans for comprehensive pediatric care.
key_facts: High-risk features include prior anaphylaxis, multi-system reactions, respiratory or cardiovascular involvement, and poorly controlled asthma.
Remote residence or limited emergency access lowers the threshold for supervised oral food challenges and extended observation.
Nutritional risks include faltering growth, hypoalbuminemia, feeding refusal, and multiple food avoidance.
Referral is indicated for severe non-IgE phenotypes (e.g., FPIES, FPE, severe FPIAP), failure to thrive, and diet expansion needs.
Optimize asthma control before elective food challenges and integrate action plans into school and home safety counseling.
reference_quote: High-risk features that should escalate emergency preparedness and referral include prior anaphylaxis or multi-system reactions, respiratory or cardiovascular involvement, need for more than one dose of IM epinephrine, and poorly controlled asthma. (Source: Food Allergy in Children, IAP Action Plan 2026)
document_key_topics: Risk stratification for pediatric food allergy
Referral thresholds for severe food allergy phenotypes
Integration of asthma and food allergy management
